elements.sent-2411-007: Contemporary information retrieval (IR) models are often either retrievers \cite[inter alia]{reimers-gurevych-2019-sentence,karpukhin-etal-2020-dense,izacard2022unsupervised that efficiently pre-compute document representations or rerankers \cite[inter alia]{Nogueira2019PassageRW,gao2021multistage,glass-etal-2022-re2g that jointly encode query--document pairs, often with cross-encoder architectures.}}
elements.sent-2411-008: These two paradigms pose a popular tradeoff between quality and cost: the highly expressive modeling of rerankers is widely understood in the literature \cite{Rosa2022InDO to boost quality and generalization, but retrievers are orders of magnitude cheaper due to pre-indexing document representations.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Modern IR systems often handle this tradeoff using multi-stage reranking pipelines \cite{matveeva2006nested,lidan2011cascade,nogueira2019multi , in which a fast retriever finds the initial top-$K$ candidate documents and a reranker then re-scores only those $K$.}
elements.sent-2411-010: If the models used are well-chosen, e.g. best-in-class retrievers and rerankers, it is generally assumed \cite[inter alia]{Humeau2020Poly-encoders,luan-etal-2021-sparse that introducing rerankers will consistently improve overall quality and that increasing the number of reranked documents $K$ will progressively lead to even higher gains.}
elements.sent-2411-011: As a corollary, using rerankers to score the whole document set should also be an effective, though perhaps unrealistically expensive, way to get high recall.

elements.sent-2411-014: As a consequence, modern rerankers frequently perform worse than retrievers when ranking the full dataset (Figure~\ref{fig:simple_fig_full ).}
elements.sent-2411-015: Qualitative analyses show that rerankers often get distracted by documents with minimal lexical and semantic overlap with the query; we find that listwise reranking via large language models presents a possible path forward to more robust reranking.

elements.sent-2411-103: In the previous section, we saw that the chosen rerankers consistently degrade as we scale the number of documents for reranking.
elements.sent-2411-104: Importantly, we were experimenting exclusively with pointwise cross-encoder rerankers.
elements.sent-2411-105: We now attempt a relatively recent technique: listwise reranking using large language models.
elements.sent-2411-106: In this approach, the documents are inserted into the prompt, using a sliding window to handle context length constraints, and the LLM generates an ordering of their relevance to the input query.
elements.sent-2411-107: Despite not being directly finetuned for this task, the LLM could be expected to do well given its general text generation capabilities and because listwise ranking incorporates more context than pointwise ranking.
elements.sent-2411-108: We base our listwise reranking strategy on the sliding window prompting approach from \citet{sun-etal-2023-chatgpt using a window size of 20, stride size of 10, and gpt-4o-mini as the LLM.\footnote{Anecdotally, we found Llama-3.1-70B yielded similar results as gpt-4o-mini, although a full exploration of LLM performance is outside the scope of this study.}}
elements.sent-2411-109: The window starts with the least relevant documents provided by the first stage.
elements.sent-2411-110: When our program fails to parse the output of the LLM due to syntax or logical errors (e.g. a repeated document ID), then we simply keep the first stage ordering for that window.\footnote{Errors like this occur about 5\% of the time when $K=100$, aligning with previous reports \cite{pradeep2023rankzephyr .}}
elements.sent-2411-111: When conducting these listwise experiments, ``Failed to parse'' errors occured sparingly for most datasets, with the highest failure usually occuring when retrieving 1000 documents with a failure rate of about 10\% of LLM calls.
elements.sent-2411-112: On FinanceBench, we saw a maximum error rate of 19\% of LLM calls.
elements.sent-2411-113: When inspecting the outputs from the model on failed windows, we observe that almost all of the errors occurred when the model thought that none of the documents being ranked were relevant to the query, and thus either didn't follow the format specified in the prompt or refused to output a ranking altogether.

elements.sent-2411-116: The results indicate that the listwise reranker is both more robust and higher quality compared to the other rerankers.
elements.sent-2411-117: Based on these results, we believe that LLMs as rerankers may be a possible path forward, given that they are more robust to document scale than the cross-encoder rerankers analyzed in the previous section.

elements.sent-2411-119: The primary finding of this paper is that rerankers (i.e. pointwise cross-encoders) yield surprisingly worse results when reranking many documents.
elements.sent-2411-120: In this section, we speculate on three possible causes of the observed behavior: insufficient exposure to negatives during training, the main benefit of rerankers being an ensembling effect, and the limited robustness of deep learning.
elements.sent-2411-121: We conjecture that one cause of today’s point-wise rerankers lack of robustness may be exposure bias with respect to how rerankers are exposed to a limited set of negatives during training.
elements.sent-2411-122: Pointwise rerankers may see less negatives in training than their embedding model counterparts due to how much more computationally expensive it is to train on a batch for rerankers compared to embedding models.
elements.sent-2411-123: Thus, we hypothesize that rerankers are trained on negatives selected from a subset of the corpus filtered by retrievers, which may explain the peak performance of rerankers occuring at a lower number of retrieved documents, where the documents the reranker scores more closely resemble its training data.
elements.sent-2411-124: Thus, while it is theoretically intuitive for rerankers to be considered strictly better models than embedding models, the computational expense of fully training a reranker is prohibitive enough to prevent this from being the case today.
elements.sent-2411-125: For example, when training the mGTE embedding and reranking model in \cite{zhang2024mgte , the authors use 16,384 randomly selected in-batch negatives to train their embedding model, while only using 4 randomly selected negatives to train their cross-attention based reranker model.}
elements.sent-2411-126: Additionally, some approaches to training embedding models are able to scale to millions of negative documents \cite{xiong2020approximate, lindgren2021efficient .}
elements.sent-2411-127: Under-trained rerankers may explain why previous research has shown embeddings can match cross-encoder quality \cite{menon22defense .}
elements.sent-2411-128: It's confusing that rerankers can help when we've shown that they are overall worse than retrievers at tasks like full retrieval (\S\ref{sec:full_rerank ).}
elements.sent-2411-129: It's also hard to understand how they can be worse than retrievers when a cross-encoder and dense embedding are essentially the same model architecture, but trained with different input data.
elements.sent-2411-130: Perhaps these observations make more sense if we view reranking as a type of ensembling, where the reranker is providing alternative features that are helpful to discriminate relevant from irrelevant documents only from the top of the list.
elements.sent-2411-131: Diversity is a useful property in ensembles \cite{Maclin1999PopularEM , and the nature of reranker training is that computation constraints make it infeasible to train with similar data as retrievers when using a similar budget.}
elements.sent-2411-132: This would explain why rerankers show great improvement on top of retrievers when retrieving a smaller number of documents, as rerankers are specialized at accurately scoring the type of documents that are given to it when $k$ is small.
elements.sent-2411-133: Scaling laws for dense retrieval explain how retrieval quality may improve with an increase in model parameters and training data \cite{Fang2024ScalingLF , although they do not factor in reranking.}

elements.sent-2411-135: Representative instances of this problem include vision models failing to classify images due to small perturbations \cite{deeplearningvisionrobustness , as well as text processing models changing their prediction when substituting words in their input with synonyms \cite{nlprobustness .}}
elements.sent-2411-136: In the context of reranking, as one scales the number of documents, each additional document included creates a risk that the model may assign an inappropriately high score to an irrelevant document.

elements.sent-2411-138: For example, Best-of-N decoding can suffer with large N due to reward hacking, which leads to finding a more favorable proxy reward that doesn't necessarily improve results on the downstream task \cite{learn2summarize,Nakano2021WebGPTBQ,Pan2022TheEO,gao2023rm,Lambert2023TheAC,rafailov2024scaling .}
elements.sent-2411-139: The lack of robustness in rerankers is exacerbated when they assign scores independently to each document (pointwise).
elements.sent-2411-140: Using models that are trained with a listwise loss or listwise inference procedure has been shown to improve ranking quality \cite{ai2018listwise,ai2019listwise,rahimi2019listwise,Gao2023PolicyGradientTO .}
elements.sent-2411-141: This is one explanation why listwise-reranking with large language models outperformed pointwise-reranking with cross-encoders in \S\ref{sec:listwise .}
elements.sent-2411-142: In general, the research community has highlighted the challenges associated with finetuning.
elements.sent-2411-143: One explanation for why our listwise-reranker may outperform the cross-encoder rerankers is that the listwise-reranker is based on gpt-4o-mini without any finetuning.
elements.sent-2411-144: The rerankers, on the otherhand, have almost definitely been finetuned for the reranking task, which may have lead to catastrophic forgetting or other modeling issues.
elements.sent-2411-145: To emphasize, although the shortcomings of current finetuning recipes are well established \cite{loradatabricks , finetuning for ranking presents even further challenges since this procedures is used to convert models from text generation to embedding \cite{llm2vec or classification.}}
elements.sent-2411-146: We empirically study how, in modern information retrieval systems, scaling the inference compute of different rerankers impacts the quality of the retrieved output.
elements.sent-2411-147: We do this by testing modern OSS and closed source embedding models and rerankers on carefully curated academic and enterprise datasets.
elements.sent-2411-148: We find that, for modern cross-encoders, scaling the inference compute by reranking more documents ultimately leads to significant performance degredation on Recall.
elements.sent-2411-149: Furthermore, for full retrieval (without reranking), we find that modern embedding models outperform cross-encoders.
elements.sent-2411-150: As a path forward, we present evidence that using large language for listwise reranking can outperform cross-encoders, making this listwise approach a potential teacher to improve cross-encoders or serve directly as a reranker when the cost-quality tradeoff is desirable.
elements.sent-2411-151: We hope our findings and analyses will be a useful resources to practitioners as they deploy reranker pipelines, as well as spur future research to improve cross-encoder rerankers.

elements.sent-2411-160: For the datasets, we either downsampled the corpus size, number of queries, or made gold labels stricter, which we shall explain in more detail.
elements.sent-2411-161: When downsampling the size of the corpus, we made sure the maximum size of the corpus was $10,000$ documents.
elements.sent-2411-162: To construct this new corpus, we first added all of the gold documents from the queries into the corpus to make sure those queries could be correctly answered.
elements.sent-2411-163: Then, we randomly sampled from the larger corpus without replacement to choose the remaining documents.
elements.sent-2411-164: This corpus downsampling was done for BRIGHT's biology split.
elements.sent-2411-165: When downsampling the queries, we simply randomly selected the new queries from the main dataset.
elements.sent-2411-166: This downsampling was done for Scifact.
elements.sent-2411-167: For the BRIGHT datasets, we use the `documents' split and the gemini-generated reasoning queries, as this type of query reformulation was deemed to work more effectively in the BRIGHT paper.
elements.sent-2411-168: For the BIRCO datasets, the gold documents are densely labeled with `qrel' scores, which is used to gauge how relevant a given gold document is to the query.
elements.sent-2411-169: The higher the qrel score, the more relevant that gold document is.
elements.sent-2411-170: In order to accomodate the binarization of relevance judgement scores that recall requires, we create a new dataset such that all the new gold documents are the gold documents of the original dataset that share the highest qrel score for a given query.

Mutations in TRPML1 lead to mucolipidosis type IV, a severe lysosomal storage disorder. Here we report two electron cryo-microscopy structures of full-length human TRPML1: a 3.72-A apo structure at pH 7.0 in the closed state, and a 3.49-A agonist-bound structure at pH 6.0 in an open state. Several aromatic and hydrophobic residues in pore helix 1, helices S5 and S6, and helix S6 of a neighbouring subunit, form a hydrophobic cavity to house the agonist, suggesting a distinct agonist-binding site from that found in TRPV1, a TRP channel from a different subfamily. The opening of TRPML1 is associated with distinct dilations of its lower gate together with a slight structural movement of pore helix 1. elements.sent-2411-178: Rerankers, typically cross-encoders, are computationally intensive but are frequently used because they are widely assumed to outperform cheaper initial IR systems.
elements.sent-2411-179: To provide a more robust evaluation, we prioritize strong first-stage retrieval using modern dense embeddings and test rerankers on a variety of carefully chosen, challenging tasks, including internally curated datasets to avoid contamination, and out-of-domain ones.
elements.sent-2411-180: Our empirical results reveal a surprising trend: the best existing rerankers provide initial improvements when scoring progressively more documents, but their effectiveness gradually declines and can even degrade quality beyond a certain limit.
elements.sent-2411-181: Contemporary information retrieval (IR) models are often either retrievers \cite[inter alia]{reimers-gurevych-2019-sentence,karpukhin-etal-2020-dense,izacard2022unsupervised which pre-compute document representations for efficient retrieval or rerankers \cite[inter alia]{Nogueira2019PassageRW,gao2021multistage,glass-etal-2022-re2g that jointly encode query--document pairs, often with cross-encoder architectures.}}
elements.sent-2411-182: These two paradigms pose a popular tradeoff between quality and cost: retrievers are orders of magnitude cheaper as they pre-index document representations, and while rerankers are more expensive, they are widely understood in the literature to boost quality and generalization due to their expressive modeling capabilities~\cite{Rosa2022InDO .}
elements.sent-2411-183: Modern IR systems often manage this tradeoff using multi-stage reranking pipelines \cite{matveeva2006nested,lidan2011cascade,nogueira2019multi , where a fast retriever identifies the initial top-$K$ candidate documents and a reranker then re-scores only those $K$.}
elements.sent-2411-184: If the models used are well-chosen---e.g. best-in-class retrievers and rerankers---it is generally assumed both (1) that introducing rerankers will consistently improve overall quality and (2) that increasing the number of reranked documents $K$ will progressively lead to even higher gains~\cite[inter alia]{Humeau2020Poly-encoders,luan-etal-2021-sparse .}
elements.sent-2411-185: As a corollary, using rerankers to score the entire document set should also be an effective---albeit potentially unrealistic in terms of cost---approach for achieving high recall.
elements.figure-2411-031: \begin{subfigure}{0.48\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{plots_v2/simple-rerank-black.pdf} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.48\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures_tex/phantom_hit_fig.pdf} \end{subfigure} \caption{Left: While rerankers are thought to outperform retrievers, we find instances where scaling the number of documents $K$ used in reranking often leads to a substantial decrease in recall. Right: Our definition of phantom hit, which is an unexpected error we observed in various neural rerankers.} \label{fig:simple_fig_rerank}
elements.sent-2411-186: We test these seemingly innocuous assumptions using best-in-class rerankers across several underexplored public and enterprise IR benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-187: Notably, our choice of datasets and models departs from conventional reranker research, which typically focuses on BM25 for first-stage retrieval and MSMarco for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-188: We find that these assumptions are frequently false.
elements.sent-2411-189: Specifically, while rerankers initially help with small values for $K$, reranking with large $K$ decreases recall precipitously (Figure~\ref{fig:simple_fig_rerank ), often dropping beneath the quality of standalone retrievers.}
elements.sent-2411-190: As a consequence, modern rerankers frequently perform worse than retrievers when both rank the full dataset.
elements.sent-2411-191: Upon studying this, we find that rerankers often score completely irrelevant documents, lacking semantic or lexical similarity to the query, very highly.
elements.sent-2411-192: These same documents, which we call phantom hits, are often given very low scores by the retrievers, revealing a counterintuitive way in which retriever models can outperform reranker models in practice.
elements.sent-2411-193: Our main findings are thus that (1) we empirically demonstrate that scaling K leads to a substantial decrease in recall in multiple settings, (2) we qualitatively reveal and investigate a pathology in reranking that we describe as Phantom Hits, where the reranker assigns a high score to documents that have no lexical or semantic overlap with the query, and (3) we demonstrate a proof-of-existence that more robust reranking options exist and can be implemented with large language models (LLMs).
elements.sent-2411-194: Overall, we establish that the current understanding of rerankers does not match their behavior in practice and call for more research to improve their robustness to noise.

elements.subsection-2411-010: Does scaling the number of documents for reranking help? \label{sec:scaling_helps}
elements.sent-2411-209: In the majority of cases, we see that reranking a small set of documents is an effective way to improve recall on a dataset (Figures~\ref{fig:recall_10_plots and \ref{fig:recall_10_plots_enterprise ).}}
elements.sent-2411-210: This claim is reinforced by the statistics from individual experiments,\footnote{An experiment is uniquely identified by a (dataset, retriever, reranker) tuple.}
elements.sent-2411-211: The full results for individual experiments are included in Appendix~\ref{app:detailed_results .}
elements.sent-2411-212: A closer look at the results reveals that even though rerankers are effective when reranking a few documents, that rerankers are much less consistently helpful when scaling the number of documents for reranking.
elements.sent-2411-213: To make this more clear, in Table~\ref{tab:analysis_scaling we include a fine-grained analysis of reranker average Recall@10 at different values of K by filtering for different cases of interest.}
elements.sent-2411-214: Our initial filter (Helps) is to look for experiments where there exists at least one K such that reranker Recall@10 is greater than the retriever's.
elements.sent-2411-215: We investigate four additional filters (Helps + X):.
elements.sent-2411-216: Never Hurts: There is no K such that reranker Recall@10 is less than the retriever's.
elements.sent-2411-217: Distracted: The reranker improves over the retriever at K, but there is K' > K where the reranker's Recall@10 for K' is less than its Recall@10 for K.
elements.sent-2411-218: Scaling is Best: The reranker's maximum Recall@10 is at the maximum K.
elements.sent-2411-219: Scaling Hurts: The reranker's Recall@10 at the maximum K is worse than the retriever's.

elements.sent-2411-223: BM25 plus reranking might seem promising, but the reality is that this can be outperformed by retrieval-only with strong dense embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-224: For example, we see that text-embedding-3-large is roughly 1.5x as effective as BM25 on the enterprise data, measured by average Recall@10.
elements.sent-2411-225: Retrieving a few documents with a dense embedding would be better than reranking many documents with BM25.\footnote{Our focus on strong retrievers and challenging datasets is in contrast to previous work that relies heavily on BM25 and MS MARCO~\cite{hof2019lowfreq .}}
elements.subsubsection-2411-001: How to fairly compare retrieval and reranking
elements.sent-2411-226: Given that rerankers often show improved retrieval quality when reranking a few documents, one may assume that rerankers are more accurate than retrievers.
elements.sent-2411-227: Additionally, it's rarely clear how effective a reranker really is, since a reranker is usually evaluated jointly with first stage retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-228: With this in mind, we develop a fair and simple protocol for comparing retrievers and rerankers: Rather than apply the reranker only on the top documents provided by the retriever, apply the reranker on all of the documents that are being retrieved from.
elements.sent-2411-229: We use this protocol with our retrievers and rerankers of interest and present the results in Figure~\ref{fig:recall_k , where we plot Recall@K across all values of K.}
elements.sent-2411-230: In line with our previous findings, we see that retrievers are better than rerankers in many settings.

elements.sent-2411-232: In a larger percentage of cases we find helpful rerankers will get distracted and Recall@10 will dip at higher K, though not always falling below the retriever.
elements.sent-2411-233: It is plausible that this decrease in recall could be due to missing labels, and rerankers finding previously unknown but relevant documents.
elements.sent-2411-234: However, when manually looking through the reranker's predictions, we see that this is not the case.
elements.sent-2411-235: Instead, we attribute many reranker errors to what we call ``phantom hits''.
elements.sent-2411-236: These are cases where the reranker prefers an irrelevant negative document to the positive ground truth when there is no apparent connection between the query and irrelevant document, lexical, semantic, or otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-237: In this error analysis, we seek a few representative examples of these ``phantom hits''.
elements.sent-2411-238: We show examples of these reranker failures in Figures~\ref{fig:error_analysis_1 and \ref{fig:error_analysis_2 .}}

elements.sent-2411-260: In this section, in order to aid future research, we highlight three potential causes for unexpected failures in rerankers and how they might be addressed.
elements.sent-2411-261: We conjecture that today’s point-wise rerankers lack of robustness is partially due to exposure bias during reranking training.
elements.sent-2411-262: Namely, pointwise rerankers may see less negatives in training than their embedding model counterparts because large batch reranker training is computationally expensive and does not easily share negatives across the batch.
elements.sent-2411-263: It's similarly counter-intuitive that they can be worse than retrievers given that a cross-encoder and dense embedding are essentially the same model architecture, but trained with different input data.
